author: garyrong
content:
https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/pull/11844/changes#r3526635702

<@429719902363058177> <@968526635735326741> let two questions, please take a look

author: gaxanh
content: I don't see a case for self-transfer to 7702 delegated. This case should be similar to ETH transfer to self except for the additional cost of accessing the delegated address, meaning the price is 15k instead of higher

author: misilva73
content:
> My guess is that the 8k cost actually represents two account updates, one for the caller and one for the callee. 

No, this is not true. The `TX_BASE` covers the access and write for the caller

author: draganrakita
content: <@508125616940515329> you had a lot of question around values of TX_BASE and why can't we use values from EIP-8038

author: draganrakita
content:
Yes, this should be defined. I dont mind both option. I would say for Option 2 that journal checkpoint is easy to create so would ignore protocol simplicity argument.

Lets frame it like this, in future if we have new tx that does state change (for example after or before eip7702) what would we expect if we dont have enought gas. I would say revert is better as atomicity of tx is preserved.

author: misilva73
content: I would also lean towards option 1

author: misilva73
content: <@429719902363058177> , did another round of comments and answered a coupld of questions

author: misilva73
content: Are we ok to sort all the remaining questions? Or do we need to bring up anything else on ACDT?

author: draganrakita
content: Going through the comments, but i think most of them are clarifications. Only open question is what to do if create target is already existing, pavel raised this for frame [here](https://discord.com/channels/595666850260713488/1504670813339652169/1522522217152253962) but it does apply on eip2780

author: gaxanh
content: As I mentioned above, `TX_BASE` is the cost of tx.sender (caller) alone, but it's also always written in tx.sender and is understood as **half of** `ACCOUNT_WRITE` (`TX_VALUE_COST`). The `TX_BASE` analysis shows that updating tx.to is **half of** the remaining `ACCOUNT_WRITE` (and we only have a maximum of one update in the case of transferring ether at the intrinsic level). In internal tx, there are always a maximum of 2 accounts updated, so the cost is folded into `ACCOUNT_WRITE`. Did I misunderstand anything?

author: gaxanh
content: I think it's better to keep the current EIP-7702 behavior. I initially expected reverts to be atomic, but since they are not, I don't think the authorization tuples should be affected by them either.

author: gaxanh
content: I'd be in favor of removing the intrinsic costs from EIP-7702 altogether and instead charging gas lazily on each access. If execution runs out of gas, it should halt and revert all prior state changes. Assuming there are no technical objections, this is the approach I'd prefer to see.

author: draganrakita
content: I think all PR questions are addressed, pls check it again: https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/pull/11844

author: jochembrouwer
content: I'll re-read the entire EIP 🙂 Thanks for the feedback on my comments 😄

author: chfast
content: Will 8037 update follow? there is a big overlap and they contradict now.

author: jochembrouwer
content: I am sadly late for acdt. One of the points which I think make the repricing set of the EIPs confusing is the (circular?) dependency tree. 8038 should lay out the storage costs, 8037 the state creation costs, and 2780 the tx level costs. Now it seems like rules are in multiple places and contradicting each other in some cases. We should fix this by having each EIP have clear "boundaries" what it defined and what rules it "owns"

author: jochembrouwer
content: Will attempt to listen in on first half

author: misilva73
content:
> 8038 should lay out the storage costs, 8037 the state creation costs, and 2780 the tx level costs

I think this is the right breakdown. I what I had before these new changes was this, but then I would also reference the other EIPs when it made sense.

For now, let's focus on moving on the the design and then I can clean up the EIPs

author: lu_pinto
content: I share the same opinion as <@508125616940515329>  these 3 EIPs should all be a single EIP. The authorization list part also has state costs associated so I guess the intrinsic part and the state part of 7702 will now be split across 2780 and 8037

author: jochembrouwer
content: I disagree that it should be a single EIP, this would be too big and all-over-the-place. With the mentioned "focus points", these EIPs do not have circular dependencies but instead link or build on the EIPs they depend on. That would be 2780->8037->8038, so EIP-2780 can link back to EIP-8038, but EIP-8038 cannot link back to EIP-2780, it should all build upon each other

author: jochembrouwer
content: The main part is that we should remove the circular dependencies and rules in multiple EIPs, which "should be" the same rules but tend to diverge, the reason is the EIP process where you cannot update two EIPs at once

author: lu_pinto
content: thanks for clarifying, misunderstood your stance then. Still think they should be altogether - impossible to not split the authorisation costs between 2 EIPs. Anyway, this is a small nit and a waste of time discussing but just wanted to get it out of my chest 😄
